title: Day 1 · Phase 1 — Re-anchoring: what makes 2026-XJ move?
bodyHtml:
DAY 1 · Phase 1 — From velocity to force

You predicted WHERE 2026-XJ is going. Today we start asking WHY.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS (preview)

K1. Universal gravitation as the force that runs the asteroid show. R4. Why 2026-XJ doesn't drift away.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Where we are: end of Unit 1, students have a velocity-time prediction for the asteroid based on radar data.

Today's pivot: Unit 1 answered "how fast?" Unit 2 answers "why fast?" — which is the same as "what force?" The unit's load-bearing question is whether 2026-XJ's path bends enough to bring it to Earth.

Lesson Flow

(~50 min; adjust for block.)

TimeWhat happens
0–5Return Unit 1 transfer tasks. Highlight one or two strong predictions. Reframe: "Your prediction assumed velocity stays constant. It doesn't. Why?"
5–18Demo: drop a hardcover book + a flat sheet of paper at the same height, same release. Then crumple the paper, repeat. Ask the room: "Why did the rule change?" Lead to: without air resistance, all objects fall the same.
18–28Notice & Wonder — students list what they notice about the demo, what they wonder about gravity. Sort into "physics can answer" / "physics cannot answer."
28–42Mini-lecture preview: gravity is a force, and it follows a law (Newton's universal gravitation). Don't derive it yet — frame the unit's question: "Knowing the law, we can predict whether 2026-XJ's orbit bends toward us."
42–48Exit ticket: "Why doesn't 2026-XJ just drift off into space at constant velocity? What pulls on it?"
48–50Marzano conversation on K1/R4 preview.

Prep: Unit 1 transfer tasks scored; hardcover book + sheet of paper; clear floor zone; whiteboard with two columns ("physics can answer" / "can't").

Talk moves on the demo: resist the temptation to explain. Push the room: "What's the same about the book and the crumpled paper that's different about the flat paper?" Let air resistance surface from them.

Differentiation: ELs — sentence frames for the exit ticket; SWDs — written prompt + a verbal version offered; advanced — ask "if there's NO air on a different planet, what's the equivalent demo?"

title: Day 2 · Phase 1 — Free fall: everything falls at g
bodyHtml:
DAY 2 · Phase 1 — The acceleration of free fall

g = 9.81 m/s² near Earth. Why every object — coin, feather, asteroid — accelerates the same.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1. Gravity as a constant acceleration near Earth. S4. Reading g off a v-t slope.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If 2026-XJ enters Earth's gravity well close enough, it accelerates at g. We need to know what g is — and that it's the SAME for the asteroid as for a coin you drop.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Quick read-back of best Day-1 exit tickets. Anchor: "Gravity pulls everything the same." Bring receipts.
5–18Vernier motion detector demo: drop a ball, project the v-t graph. Slope = g. Repeat with a different ball — slope is the same.
18–32Worked examples on the board: How long to fall 10 m from rest? 100 m? Use v = gt and y = ½gt². Two examples fully worked.
32–45Pair practice — 4 problems on the packet. Predict-then-check using a stopwatch + drop (when safe).
45–48Exit ticket: "How long does it take a coin to fall 1 m from rest, ignoring air?"
48–50Marzano on K1.

Prep: Vernier motion detector + ball; tape measure; stopwatch for student trials; packet pages.

Talk moves: after the v-t demo, "What does the slope mean physically? What would a STEEPER slope tell you?"

Differentiation: ELs — pair a calculator-fluent partner with a language partner; advanced — "what would g be on a planet with twice Earth's mass at the same radius?" (preview Day 6).

title: Day 3 · Phase 1 — Weight as F = mg (local gravity)
bodyHtml:
DAY 3 · Phase 1 — Weight is a force; mass is universal

F = mg works HERE because g is local. The asteroid's weight on Earth ≠ its weight at lunar distance.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K2. Weight vs. mass; gravitational field strength as a local quantity.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If we put 2026-XJ on a giant scale on Earth's surface, what would it read? Set up the question — answer it after we have F = Gm₁m₂/r² in hand on Day 4.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hook: "What's your mass? What's your weight on Earth? On the Moon? On 2026-XJ?" Get class to articulate that one of these is the SAME everywhere.
5–18Direct instruction: mass is how much stuff (kg, universal). Weight is the force of gravity ON that mass (N, depends on local g). F = mg.
18–32Worked example progression: 70-kg student on Earth (g=9.81), Moon (g=1.62), Mars (g=3.71), asteroid surface (g≈0.0003). Pattern emerges: mass constant, weight scales with g.
32–45Independent practice — 6 problems on a worksheet. Mix masses, mix g values. Aspen-style answer column.
45–48Exit ticket: "If you weigh 600 N on Earth, what is your mass? What would you weigh on a planet with g = 25 m/s²?"
48–50Marzano on K2. Tomorrow: where does g COME FROM?

Prep: worksheet printed; calculators if needed; small bathroom scale as a prop ("this measures FORCE, not mass").

Talk moves: when students conflate mass and weight, push back: "You just said you'd weigh less on the Moon. Did your mass change? What did?"

Differentiation: ELs — cognate pair (masa / mass; peso / weight) on the board, refer often; SWDs — a 3-column table (planet | g | weight) to organize work; advanced — "derive Mars g from Mars's mass and radius" (preview Day 6).

title: Day 4 · Phase 2 — Newton's law of universal gravitation
bodyHtml:
DAY 4 · Phase 2 — The law that decides 2026-XJ's path

F = G m₁ m₂ / r². Every mass pulls every other mass.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1. Universal gravitation as an inverse-square law. R1. Reasoning about how F changes with mass and distance (preview).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

This is THE equation we've been building toward. Every distance the asteroid moves changes the force on it. The 1/r² geometry decides how steeply the force ramps up as it approaches.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–3Quick check: "What's mass? What's weight? Why did weight change on Mars yesterday?"
3–18Direct instruction: F = G m₁ m₂ / r². Walk through each piece. G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ (tiny — why gravity is the weakest force at human scales). The 1/r² geometry — surface of a sphere grows as r².
18–32PhET Gravity Force Lab. Vary m₁, m₂, r. Students predict-then-check: "If I double m₁, what happens to F?" "If I double r?" "Triple r?"
32–42Worked example on the board: F between Earth (6×10²⁴ kg) and a 1000-kg asteroid at 384,000 km. Get to a number, name it, contextualize it.
42–48Exit ticket: "If 2026-XJ doubles its distance from Earth, what happens to F_gravity? Triples?"
48–50Marzano on K1. Tomorrow: calculation day.

Prep: PhET Gravity Force Lab loaded on lab computers; copy of the inverse-square diagram (concentric spheres) for the board.

Talk moves: when students reach for "twice the distance, twice the change in force" — push: "Look at the EXPONENT. What does squared mean here?"

Differentiation: ELs — write the equation with words above each symbol; SWDs — pre-built table for the PhET activity; advanced — "if Earth's mass were halved AND distance doubled, what happens to F? Reason BEFORE you compute."

title: Day 5 · Phase 2 — F = Gm₁m₂/r² calculation day
bodyHtml:
DAY 5 · Phase 2 — Hands on the inverse-square law

Run the numbers. Build the intuition through repetition with variation.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S1. Computing F = Gm₁m₂/r². R1. Reasoning about how F scales with each input.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Students compute F for the actual Earth/Sun/Moon/2026-XJ combinations. Pattern: gravity dominates between BIG things at SHORT distances.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Quick recap of Day 4: G value, equation, the 1/r² geometry.
5–18Group worked example: Earth–Moon force at average separation. Get the number. "Is this big? Compare to your weight." (it's enormous)
18–35Group rotation: 5 problems, 4 minutes each. Mix Earth–Moon, Earth–Sun, Moon–Sun, Earth–asteroid at different separations. Each station has a calculator and the equation visible.
35–43Class compiles a table on the board: pair, separation, force. Pattern-finding: which pair gives the BIGGEST F? Which changes most when r doubles?
43–48Exit ticket: "If the asteroid is currently at 5 lunar distances from Earth, and it gets to 1 lunar distance, how many times larger is the force?" (Answer: 25×.)
48–50Marzano on S1, R1.

Prep: 5 station problem cards with masses + distances; calculators; whiteboard space for the class compilation.

Talk moves: after the Earth–Moon calculation, "This number is huge. So why don't we feel it pulling US into the Moon?" (Push toward: the force is on Earth as a whole, and we're in orbit with Earth.)

Differentiation: ELs — give the equation with all masses, distances, and G pre-labeled; SWDs — fewer stations, more time at each; advanced — "what's the F between two students sitting 1 m apart?" (Tiny — point: gravity is weak at small mass.)

title: Day 6 · Phase 2 — Gravitational fields — g as field strength
bodyHtml:
DAY 6 · Phase 2 — From force to field

g = GM/r². The local gravitational field at any distance from a mass.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K2. Gravitational field as F per unit mass; g = GM/r².

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Earth makes a gravitational field everywhere in space. As 2026-XJ moves through that field, the force it feels changes. The field is the bridge between "Earth's mass" and "force on the asteroid."

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Recap Day 5: F = Gm₁m₂/r².
5–15The field move: divide both sides of F = Gm₁m₂/r² by m_test. Define g(r) = GM/r² — the gravitational field strength at distance r from a mass M. Units: N/kg or m/s² (they're the same).
15–28Worked examples: g at Earth's surface (r = 6.37×10⁶ m, M = 6×10²⁴) — should be 9.8. g at ISS altitude (r = 6.77×10⁶ m). g at the Moon's orbit. g at the asteroid's current distance.
28–40Class table: altitude, g. Pattern: g(r) drops as 1/r². Half-strength altitude. 1%-strength altitude.
40–48Conceptual exit ticket: "Why is the ISS NOT 'in zero gravity' even though astronauts float?" (Answer: g there ≈ 8.7 m/s². They float because the ISS is in free fall AROUND Earth.)
48–50Marzano on K2.

Prep: Table template for the altitude–g compilation; calculator availability; a quick "floating astronaut" image to puncture the misconception.

Talk moves: when a student says "there's no gravity in space" — DON'T correct immediately. Ask: "What's the value of g at the ISS altitude? Compute it. Now is that zero?" Let the math do the work.

Differentiation: ELs — visual of concentric spheres around Earth with g written on each; SWDs — pre-tabled altitude/g rows to fill; advanced — "derive Mars's g from M_Mars and r_Mars," then compare.

title: Day 7 · Phase 2 — g varies with altitude — preview to orbits
bodyHtml:
DAY 7 · Phase 2 — Implications of g(r)

Graphing g vs. altitude. Setting up the orbit conversation that closes the unit.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K2. g(r) qualitatively + graphically. R3. Comparing g across distances and bodies.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The asteroid's distance is changing. So is g where it is. We can't use 9.81 m/s² for an asteroid that's a million kilometers from Earth — but we CAN use g(r) = GM/r².

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–3Quick recap: g = GM/r². Units: N/kg.
3–18Graph activity: Plot g vs. altitude (above Earth surface). Use Desmos or a worksheet grid. 5 points: surface, 100 km, 1000 km, 10000 km, lunar distance. Connect the curve.
18–30Reading the graph: where does g drop to half? To 10%? To 1%? Connect altitude to physical meaning (LEO, GEO, moon orbit).
30–42Bridge: weightlessness. ISS astronauts experience g ≈ 8.7 m/s². They float because they're FALLING. Free fall + horizontal motion = orbit. This is Day 13's argument; plant the seed now.
42–48Exit ticket: "At what altitude does Earth's g drop to half its surface value?" (Answer: r where GM/r² = 4.9, so r ≈ √(GM/4.9) = ~9×10⁶ m, or ~2500 km altitude.)
48–50Marzano on K2/R3. Tomorrow: measure g in the lab.

Prep: Graph templates or Desmos pre-loaded on lab computers; an ISS image to anchor the orbit preview.

Talk moves on weightlessness: Push hard on the misconception. "If the ISS were ACTUALLY in zero gravity, what would happen to it? Would it stay in orbit?" The answer (it'd fly off in a straight line) sets up Newton's cannon on Day 13.

Differentiation: ELs — provide the graph axes pre-labeled with units; SWDs — partner up for the graph; advanced — "what altitude gives g = g_Moon (1.62)?"

title: Day 8 · Phase 2 — Vernier free-fall lab: measure g
bodyHtml:
DAY 8 · Phase 2 — Experiment confirms theory

Vernier motion detector + ball drop. Capture v-t. Compute g from slope. Compare to 9.81.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S4. Use a Vernier motion detector to measure g. K1. Consolidate.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

We've been using g = 9.81 m/s² for a week. Today we MEASURE it. If the lab number matches theory, the F = Gm₁m₂/r² framework we're using for 2026-XJ is on solid ground.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Lab procedure overview + safety beat (ball drop, watch heads + feet, clear floor zone).
5–15Demo run by teacher: motion detector pointed up, ball dropped above it, v-t graph projected. Class identifies slope = g.
15–35Lab: pairs run 3 trials. Each trial: capture v-t, fit line to the falling portion, record slope. Take class average. Compare to 9.81.
35–43Sources of error discussion: detection angle, ball wobble, air resistance, sample rate. Which matters most?
43–48Lab write-up start (data table + claim-evidence-reasoning paragraph). Homework finish.
48–50Marzano on S4.

Prep: Vernier motion detectors + LabQuest interfaces; balls (4-cm diameter or larger work well); clear floor zones; lab write-up template; spreadsheet/Logger Pro pre-loaded.

Talk moves on the class average vs. 9.81: if you're 3% off, push: "What in our setup would cause us to read LOW? HIGH?"

Differentiation: ELs — partner with a Logger Pro-fluent student; SWDs — pre-assign roles (driver, scribe, time-keeper) per pair; advanced — "compute g + uncertainty" using the standard deviation of trial slopes.

title: Day 9 · Phase 3 — Projectile motion: two independent dimensions
bodyHtml:
DAY 9 · Phase 3 — Horizontal + vertical, separately

Gravity acts vertically only. Horizontal motion is unchanged.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. Projectile motion decomposes into independent horizontal and vertical components.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

2026-XJ doesn't travel in a straight line — it curves under gravity. Projectile motion is the small-scale, local-gravity version of what an asteroid does at planetary scale.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Frame: "For the next three days, we explore curved paths under gravity. Everything we learn here scales up to orbits."
5–18Monkey-and-hunter sim or thought experiment. Drop a ball while pushing it sideways at the same instant a second ball drops from rest. They hit the ground at the SAME TIME. Why?
18–28Direct instruction: horizontal motion = constant velocity (vₓ stays vₓ). Vertical motion = free fall (vᵧ accelerates at -g). The two are INDEPENDENT. Vector decomposition refresher from Unit 1.
28–42Worked example: horizontal launch from a 5-m cliff at vₓ = 10 m/s. How long to hit ground? How far does it land? Walk through both calculations.
42–48Quick pair practice: 2 horizontal-launch problems.
48–50Marzano on K3.

Prep: PhET Projectile Motion sim loaded; the monkey-and-hunter applet bookmarked; vector-decomposition reference page from Unit 1 visible.

Talk moves on the monkey-and-hunter result: "Why didn't the side-pushed ball take LONGER to fall?" Resist explaining; let the room work toward "because gravity acts on the vertical component only."

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing horizontal & vertical vectors with arrows; SWDs — packet with the equation pair pre-printed; advanced — "what about a 30° launch?" (Tease tomorrow.)

title: Day 10 · Phase 3 — Horizontal projectile launches
bodyHtml:
DAY 10 · Phase 3 — Cliff-style projectile problems

Vary launch height + horizontal speed. Predict landing distance.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R2. Predict projectile landing given launch conditions.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Imagine 2026-XJ traveling horizontally past Earth at high speed. Does it strike? The answer depends on the BALANCE between horizontal speed and how much it falls during flyby. (Preview of orbits.)

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Quick check: yesterday's two-cliff problem. Anyone want to put it up?
5–15Direct instruction recap: vᵧ at time t = -g·t. y(t) = y₀ - ½g·t². x(t) = vₓ·t. Three equations on the board to reference.
15–35Group problem-solving rotation. 4 stations, 4-5 min each: vary cliff height (5, 20, 50, 100 m) and launch speed (5, 10, 20 m/s). Each group solves one, then rotates.
35–43PhET Projectile Motion: vary horizontal launch speed, watch landing distance change. Confirm group answers.
43–48Exit ticket: "From a 45-m cliff at 15 m/s horizontal, where does it land?"
48–50Marzano.

Prep: 4 station cards with cliff heights and launch speeds; PhET Projectile Motion loaded; reference equation card visible at every table.

Talk moves: when a group rushes to plug numbers, "Stop. Which equation gives you TIME first? You can't get x without t."

Differentiation: ELs — visual of the cliff with labeled axes; SWDs — equation order pre-numbered (1. find t. 2. find x); advanced — "reverse the problem: I see it land 30 m away from a 20-m cliff. What was vₓ?"

title: Day 11 · Phase 3 — Angled projectile launches
bodyHtml:
DAY 11 · Phase 3 — Adding a launch angle

v decomposes into vₓ = v cos θ and vᵧ = v sin θ. 45° gives max range on level ground.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R2. Full projectile reasoning, including angled launches.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

2026-XJ doesn't approach Earth at 0° — it has SOME angle. Angled projectile math is what we'd use if it were going to enter the atmosphere.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Recap horizontal case. What's different about angled? (vᵧ ≠ 0 at launch.)
5–18Direct instruction: vₓ = v cos θ; vᵧ = v sin θ. Then the same vertical kinematics, but vᵧ starts positive instead of zero. Flight time uses vᵧ(end) = -vᵧ(start) for level ground.
18–32Worked example: 20 m/s at 30°. Compute vₓ, vᵧ, flight time, max height, range. Show every step.
32–42PhET sim: vary launch angle 0–90° at fixed speed. Class fills a table: angle vs. range. Pattern: 45° wins on level ground.
42–48Exit ticket: "Why does 45° give max range on level ground? Reason it physically, not algebraically."
48–50Marzano on R2.

Prep: PhET Projectile Motion ready; calculator + trig tables for partners; equation reference card.

Talk moves on the 45° result: when a student says "because 45 is in the middle of 0 and 90" — push: "Why does the middle work? What does angle do to the two components separately?"

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing v decomposed at multiple angles; SWDs — pre-built sin/cos table for common angles; advanced — "what's the OPTIMAL angle to launch from a CLIFF? Greater or less than 45°?"

title: Day 12 · Phase 4 — Circular motion + centripetal force
bodyHtml:
DAY 12 · Phase 4 — The force that keeps things turning

a_c = v²/r. F_c = m v²/r. Always points toward the center.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S3. Compute centripetal force and acceleration for circular motion.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

An orbit is circular motion. The force that keeps a satellite in orbit IS gravity, providing the centripetal force. Today we get the centripetal math; tomorrow we connect it to gravity.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hook: spin a ball on a string. Cut the string. Which direction does it go? (Tangent, not outward.) Why?
5–18Direct instruction: in circular motion, velocity changes DIRECTION even at constant SPEED. That's acceleration — toward the center. a_c = v²/r. The force that causes it: F_c = m v²/r. ALWAYS points inward.
18–32Worked example: a 1500-kg car rounding a 50-m radius curve at 20 m/s. What centripetal force does friction provide? Run the numbers.
32–42Worked example 2: satellite at 400-km altitude orbiting at 7700 m/s. What centripetal acceleration? Compare to g at that altitude. Notice: they MATCH. Tease orbit logic.
42–48Exit ticket: "A ball on a 1-m string moves in a horizontal circle at 4 m/s. Mass 0.2 kg. What's the centripetal force?"
48–50Marzano on S3.

Prep: ball-on-string demo (with a stopper, not the actual ball, for safety); clear space; satellite altitude reference card.

Talk moves on "string cuts → tangent": resist explaining. Ask: "What force was pulling the ball inward? When you cut the string, what happens to that force?"

Differentiation: ELs — pre-built free-body diagram with the centripetal arrow labeled; SWDs — equation written + units circled; advanced — "what if the car's speed doubled? How does the friction requirement change?"

title: Day 13 · Phase 4 — Newton's cannon: orbits as continuous falling
bodyHtml:
DAY 13 · Phase 4 — An orbit is FALLING around something

Throw it fast enough, and the surface curves away as fast as the object falls.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K5. An orbit is continuous free fall around a body.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

2026-XJ has an orbit around the Sun. The Earth's orbit and 2026-XJ's orbit cross — or they don't. If they cross at the same TIME, that's the impact question. The framework for thinking about this is "orbits are falling."

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Quick recap centripetal force = mv²/r.
5–18Newton's cannon thought experiment. Cannon on a mountain, fires horizontally. Slow → falls into ground. Faster → falls further. Fast enough → falls AROUND the planet. That's an orbit.
18–30PhET Gravity and Orbits sim — vary satellite velocity. Slow = crash. Medium = circular orbit. Fast = elliptical, then escape. Class observes.
30–42Connect: for a CIRCULAR orbit, F_gravity = F_centripetal. Set GMm/r² = mv²/r. Solve for orbital speed v = √(GM/r). Compute for ISS altitude. (Should get ~7700 m/s, matching Day 12.)
42–48Exit ticket: "In 2-3 sentences, explain why ISS doesn't fall straight down."
48–50Marzano on K5. Tomorrow: Kepler's laws.

Prep: PhET Gravity and Orbits on lab computers; Newton's cannon image or animation for the board.

Talk moves on the cannon: "What happens if I fire it twice as fast as orbit speed? Three times?" (Elliptical, then escape — that's escape velocity. Foreshadow.)

Differentiation: ELs — visual of orbit as curved fall; SWDs — explicit derivation steps for v = √(GM/r); advanced — "what's escape velocity from Earth's surface?"

title: Day 14 · Phase 4 — Kepler's three laws
bodyHtml:
DAY 14 · Phase 4 — Kepler's empirical laws of orbits

Ellipses. Equal areas. T² ∝ r³. The constraints every orbit obeys.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K4. State and apply Kepler's three laws.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If we know 2026-XJ's orbit shape and one period, Kepler's third law tells us EVERYTHING about its timing — including when it next crosses Earth's path.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hook: Kepler watched the stars BEFORE Newton invented calculus. He found patterns. Three of them.
5–15Law 1: Orbits are ellipses, with the central body at ONE FOCUS (not the center). Draw an ellipse on the board. Define foci, perihelion, aphelion.
15–24Law 2: Equal areas in equal times. Implication: orbits speed up at perihelion, slow at aphelion. Demo with a sketch.
24–35Law 3: T² ∝ r³ for objects orbiting the SAME central body. Show with Earth-Moon vs. ISS-Earth (same central body). Worked example: given semi-major axis, find period.
35–45Practice: use Kepler's third law to find orbital period for 3 different orbits around the Sun (Earth, Mars, an asteroid at a given semi-major axis).
45–48Exit ticket: "Write each of Kepler's three laws in one sentence in your own words."
48–50Marzano on K4.

Prep: Ellipse drawing on the board (with foci marked); Kepler's-third-law worksheet with practice; semi-major axis values for Mars + a sample asteroid.

Talk moves on Law 2: after stating equal-areas, ask: "What does this say about the SPEED of the orbiting object near perihelion vs. aphelion?" Let them reason to "faster when close."

Differentiation: ELs — labeled ellipse diagram with all vocabulary on it; SWDs — Law 3 worksheet with steps numbered; advanced — "why does Law 3 only work for orbits around the SAME central body?"

title: Day 15 · Phase 5 — When does 2026-XJ arrive?
bodyHtml:
DAY 15 · Phase 5 — The unit pays off

Use Kepler + universal gravitation to compute 2026-XJ's closest approach and timing.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R4. Apply gravitation + orbits to predict closest approach (load-bearing for the unit).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

This is the day we answer the year-long question — at least for now — with the tools of Unit 2: where is 2026-XJ going to be, and when?

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Frame: "Today is the asteroid math day. We have the tools. We have the question."
5–18Class is given 2026-XJ orbital parameters: semi-major axis, eccentricity, current position. Walk through what each parameter means, on the board.
18–38Group computation: Period (Kepler 3). Aphelion + perihelion distances (from semi-major + eccentricity). Will perihelion bring it inside Earth's orbit? When (year + month)? Each group works one piece, then class assembles.
38–46Compare today's prediction to Unit 1's prediction (which was just position-time extrapolation). What's different? Why is this one MORE trustworthy?
46–48Quick gather — who got what answer? Compare across groups. Discuss any discrepancies.
48–50Marzano on R4. Tomorrow: transfer task.

Prep: 2026-XJ parameter card (semi-major axis, eccentricity, current position from Unit 1's data); calculators; the Unit 1 prediction posted for comparison.

Talk moves on "why is this more trustworthy than Unit 1's?": push: "What assumption did Unit 1's prediction make? What do we know now that we didn't then?" (Unit 1 assumed constant velocity. Now we know acceleration depends on r.)

Differentiation: ELs — pre-built table for each calculation; SWDs — explicit step-by-step worksheet; advanced — "how sensitive is your closest-approach number to the eccentricity? If e were off by 0.01, how much does perihelion change?"

title: Day 16 · Phase 5 — Unit 2 transfer task
bodyHtml:
DAY 16 · Phase 5 — Assessment of learning

4 multi-part problems integrating gravity, projectile motion, circular motion, orbits. 4-dimension rubric.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 2 targets, surfaced through transfer problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The transfer task ANCHORS in the asteroid: one of the 4 problems asks students to compute F_gravity between Earth and 2026-XJ at a specific separation and reason about how that force changes.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Brief reminder of the 4-dimension rubric (Identify / Calculate / Construct / Transfer). Lowest-dimension grading.
5–45Transfer task. Independent. 4 multi-part problems: (1) F_gravity Earth–asteroid + reasoning about how F changes at half the distance; (2) projectile launched at 25° from a 30-m cliff at 18 m/s — full kinematics; (3) ISS orbit altitude given orbit speed; (4) Kepler's third law on a Sun-orbiting asteroid given semi-major axis.
45–48Final Marzano conversation on the unit target list.
48–50Growth chart updated. Look ahead: Unit 3 begins tomorrow.

Prep: transfer task printed; calculators; equation/constants reference card; quiet testing environment; growth chart pages.

Differentiation on the transfer task: ELs — vocabulary glossary attached (force, field, projectile, orbit, period); SWDs — option to verbal-explain dimension 4 (Transfer) of any problem; advanced — bonus prompt: "Given everything you've learned, predict ONE thing about 2026-XJ's orbit that you couldn't have predicted at the end of Unit 1."

Closing: Growth chart picked up tomorrow at start of Unit 3.
